lesson.name: Biostatistics — Multiple Practitioner Voices - Lesson 2 — Mike's Biostatistics Book (Dohm)

tasks.chapterTitle: Experimental Design, Probability and Inferential Statistics
Categorical Data, Quantitative Tests, Power Analysis and ANOVA
Parametric Assumptions, ANOVA Designs and Nonparametric Tests
Multiple Linear Regression and Distribution-free methods

messages.content:
This summary provides an overview of *Mike’s Biostatistics Book* by Dohm, which serves as a foundation for a semester-long course in applied biostatistics.

### **Course Overview and Philosophy**
*   **Applied Focus:** The text emphasizes the practical application of statistics, focusing on concepts, context, and clear advice rather than abstract theory alone (p. 1).
*   **Data-Driven Learning:** Examples are drawn from real-world biological and biomedical research datasets, ensuring students gain experience with practical data science skills alongside statistical theory (p. 1).
*   **Active Learning:** The material includes comprehension-testing questions to help students evaluate their understanding of the concepts (p. 1).

### **Curriculum Structure**
The textbook is organized into 20 chapters that progress from foundational principles to advanced modeling:

*   **Foundations (Chapters 1–4):** Getting started, introduction to the field, data exploration techniques, and reporting statistical findings (p. 2, 3).
*   **Design & Probability (Chapters 5–7):** Experimental design principles, probability, and risk analysis (p. 3, 4).
*   **Inferential Statistics & Comparisons (Chapters 8–10, 15):** Inferential methods, categorical data analysis, two-sample quantitative tests, and nonparametric alternatives (p. 4, 5, 6).
*   **ANOVA & Model Assumptions (Chapters 11–14):** Power analysis, one-way and multi-factor Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), and the assumptions underlying parametric testing (p. 5, 6).
*   **Regression & Relationships (Chapters 16–18):** Correlation, similarity indices, linear regression, and multiple linear regression (p. 7, 8).
*   **Advanced Topics (Chapters 19–20):** Distribution-free methods and additional specialized topics (p. 8, 9).

Lesson 2 — Mike's Biostatistics Book (Dohm) - Presentation9 slides
Mike's Biostatistics Book
  • Focuses on concepts, context, and applications.
  • Integrates data science skills with statistics.
  • Features real-world biological and biomedical datasets.

This document outlines the content and purpose of "Mike’s Biostatistics Book" by Dohm, a resource designed for an introductory semester-long course in biostatistics.

**Core Objectives and Focus**
* The text emphasizes applied statistics, focusing on conceptual understanding, context, and practical application.
* It provides a comprehensive introduction to general linear models (p. 1).
* The material connects biostatistics to broader data science skill sets as necessary for the student (p. 1).

**Methodology and Structure**
* Learning is facilitated through real-world examples derived from biological and biomedical research data, which students engage with throughout the semester (p. 1).
* Theoretical concepts are reinforced through practical practice and multiple-choice questions designed to assess reader comprehension (p. 1).
* The curriculum is organized into foundational stages, starting with introductory topics and data exploration (p. 2). 

The resource acts as a structured guide for students to master both the theoretical basis and the practical implementation of statistical models using actual research datasets (p. 1-2).

This document provides an overview of foundational statistical topics covered in Mike Dohm's biostatistics textbook. The content is organized into the following key areas:

Core Statistical Foundations
The early sections focus on the essential preliminary steps for statistical analysis. This includes the initial process of getting started with the subject (p. 2), providing a general introduction to the field (p. 2), and the methods required for exploring and understanding datasets (p. 2).

Research Methodology and Reporting
The text bridges the gap between raw data and scientific communication by covering critical procedural steps. It details the professional standards for reporting statistical findings (p. 3) and explains the principles of experimental design (p. 3) necessary to conduct rigorous biostatistical research.

The provided text outlines the structure of a biostatistics curriculum covering essential methodological and analytical topics.

Key points include:

Reporting and Design: The curriculum begins by addressing the standards for reporting statistical findings and the foundational principles of experimental design (S.3).

Probability and Analysis: It progresses into the study of probability theory and various probability distributions (S.4).

Risk and Inference: The final components cover the application of probability in risk analysis and the implementation of inferential statistics to draw conclusions from data (S.4).

### Summary of Applied Statistics Topics (Section 5)

This section covers advanced statistical methodologies for analyzing various data types and conducting comparative studies. Below are the core topics included:

**1. Categorical Data Analysis**
Focuses on procedures for evaluating data that fall into discrete categories, typically using frequency counts and contingency tables to determine associations between variables.

**2. Quantitative Two-Sample Tests**
Methods for comparing the means or distributions of two independent or paired groups. These tests are essential for determining if observed differences between samples are statistically significant.

**3. Power Analysis**
The process of determining the probability that a statistical test will correctly reject a null hypothesis when the alternative is true. Key factors include sample size, effect size, and significance level ($\alpha$). The relationship is often expressed as:
$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta$$
where $\beta$ represents the probability of a Type II error.

**4. One-way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)**
A technique used to compare the means of three or more independent groups to see if at least one group mean differs from the others. It utilizes an F-statistic, calculated as:
$$F = \frac{\text{Between-group variance}}{\text{Within-group variance}}$$
This method extends the logic of two-sample t-tests to multi-group scenarios.

### Summary of Section S.6: Statistical Methodologies

This section covers essential considerations for selecting and applying the appropriate statistical procedures for data analysis. The key topics include:

**1. Assumptions of Parametric Tests**
Parametric tests rely on specific mathematical requirements regarding the underlying data. These typically include assumptions of normality, homogeneity of variance (homoscedasticity), and independence of observations. Violations of these assumptions can lead to inaccurate results or invalid conclusions.

**2. ANOVA Designs and Multiple Factors**
Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) is used to compare means across groups. This section introduces designs involving multiple factors, which allow researchers to study the interaction effects between independent variables. For example, a two-way ANOVA examines the impact of two categorical independent variables on a dependent variable $$Y$$, as represented by the model:
$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$$
Where $$\alpha_i$$ and $$\beta_j$$ are the main effects, $$(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$$ is the interaction effect, and $$\epsilon_{ijk}$$ is the error term.

**3. Nonparametric Tests**
When the assumptions for parametric tests (such as normality) are not met—or when dealing with ordinal data—nonparametric tests serve as robust alternatives. These tests make fewer assumptions about the distribution of the data (distribution-free tests) and utilize methods such as ranking rather than raw values to determine statistical significance.

This section covers foundational concepts in statistical relationships and predictive modeling. Below is a concise summary of the key points:

1. Correlation and Similarity
Correlation measures the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables. It helps determine if changes in one variable are associated with changes in another. Similarity metrics are used to quantify how close two data points or sets are to each other, often utilizing distance calculations to define these relationships.

2. Distance
Distance measures quantify the dissimilarity between observations. Common approaches include Euclidean distance, which calculates the straight-line gap between points in a multidimensional space. These metrics are essential for clustering and identifying patterns within a dataset.

3. Linear Regression
Linear regression is a statistical method used to model the relationship between a dependent variable ($Y$) and one or more independent variables ($X$). The goal is to fit a linear equation to the observed data, typically expressed as:
$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1X + \epsilon$$

In this formula, $\beta_0$ represents the intercept, $\beta_1$ is the slope coefficient indicating the change in $Y$ for a unit change in $X$, and $\epsilon$ accounts for the error term. This technique allows researchers to predict outcomes and assess the influence of specific variables on a response.

Summary of Section 8: Advanced Statistical Methods

This section covers two primary statistical topics:

1. Multiple Linear Regression: This technique extends simple linear regression by examining the relationship between a single dependent variable and two or more independent variables. It is used to generate a predictive model represented by a formula such as $$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1X_1 + \beta_2X_2 + ... + \beta_nX_n + \epsilon$$. This approach helps in understanding how various factors contribute to an outcome simultaneously while controlling for confounding variables.

2. Distribution-free Methods: Also known as non-parametric statistics, these methods are used when the data does not meet the strict assumptions of parametric tests (such as normality or equal variance). They rely on rankings or sign tests rather than the parameters of a specific distribution (like the mean or standard deviation), making them highly robust for various types of biological and social science datasets.

This section identifies the concluding elements of the provided biostatistics textbook. Here is a summary of the key points:

Back Matter and Structural Information
The section corresponds to index S.9 (pages 20 and following), which covers supplemental material, the book's appendix, and additional topics.

Visual Documentation
The cover imagery featured in the textbook is identified as a manowar fractal. This graphic was created using the software Fractal Explorer in conjunction with the GNU Image Manipulation Program (GIMP).

Digital Metadata
The document is part of a biostatistics resource hosted on the LibreTexts platform. It provides a timestamp for the most recent update, which was recorded on May 11, 2026.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Based on the description of 'Mike's Biostatistics Book,' what is the primary pedagogical goal of the text?
options: To provide comprehensive memorization of statistical formulas and proofs.
To emphasize concepts, context, and practical application using real biological data.
To focus exclusively on theoretical mathematics without software usage.
To prepare students for pure academic research independent of data science skills.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states the focus is on concepts, context, and practical advice, using real data sets to illustrate application rather than rote memorization.

questions.question: Why does this book incorporate real data sets from biological or biomedical research?
options: To ensure that students perform manual calculations for every statistical test.
To align the curriculum with common data science techniques.
To provide context and practical experience that links statistical models to real-world inquiry.
To minimize the need for understanding probability distributions.
questions.explanation: The text notes that real data sets are used to provide the context and practice necessary for students to understand how statistics apply to biological research.

questions.question: Given the progression of topics (e.g., from 'One-way Analysis of Variance' to 'Multiple Factors' and 'Linear Regression'), which overarching framework does the author imply is foundational to the course?
options: General linear models
Manual algebraic computation
Nonparametric data manipulation
Purely descriptive reporting
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions providing a 'generous introduction to general linear models,' which serves as the unifying framework for regression and ANOVA topics in the book.

questions.question: If a student were struggling to understand the 'Assumptions of Parametric Tests' (Chapter 13), where should they look for foundational context provided earlier in the book?
options: Chapter 19: Distribution-free methods
Chapter 6: Probability and Distributions
Chapter 18: Multiple Linear Regression
Chapter 20: Additional Topics
questions.explanation: Understanding parametric assumptions requires knowledge of probability distributions, which are covered in Chapter 6 before addressing test assumptions in Chapter 13.

questions.question: How should a student approach the 'multiple choice questions' embedded within the book chapters?
options: As a standardized exam that measures absolute grades.
As a way to test and verify their comprehension of the concepts.
As an alternative to working with real data sets.
As the primary source of the book's theoretical definitions.
questions.explanation: The text identifies the multiple-choice questions as a tool to 'test' reader comprehension, serving as a check for understanding rather than just a grading mechanism.

cards.front: What is the primary focus of 'Mike's Biostatistics Book'?
cards.back: The book focuses on applied statistics, specifically concepts, context, and practical advice for a first course in Biostatistics, including a generous introduction to general linear models.

cards.front: Where are the examples and data sets for the lessons derived from?
cards.back: Examples are derived from real-world data sets in biological or biomedical research.

cards.front: What are the three core areas stressed throughout the text?
According to the book, what is the importance of using real data sets?
cards.back: Real data sets provide the necessary context and practice to ensure students understand the application of statistical methods.

cards.front: What specific modeling approach is heavily introduced in this book?
How does the author test the reader's comprehension throughout the lessons?
cards.back: Through the use of illustrative examples and multiple-choice questions.

cards.front: Are the statistical concepts taught in isolation or contextually?
cards.back: They are taught contextually, often with reference to other data science skill sets as needed.

cards.front: Which broad categories of data analysis are covered in the later chapters (Chapters 9-15)?
cards.back: Categorical data, two-sample tests, power analysis, ANOVA, assumptions of parametric tests, and non-parametric tests.

cards.front: What does the text cover regarding regression analysis?
cards.back: Simple linear regression and multiple linear regression.

cards.front: Besides standard statistical tests, what does the curriculum include for data relationships?

slides.title: Mike's Biostatistics Book
bullets: Focuses on concepts, context, and applications.
Integrates data science skills with statistics.
Features real-world biological and biomedical datasets.
slides.notes: This course covers the fundamentals of biostatistics through an applied, practical lens. We will use real biological data to bridge the gap between theory and research practice.

slides.title: Data and Foundations
slides.notes: We begin by establishing a baseline, introducing the core concepts, and learning how to visualize and explore biological data sets.

slides.title: Reporting and Design
bullets: How to report statistical findings accurately.
slides.notes: Beyond calculation, we look at how to properly document results and structure experiments to ensure valid statistical conclusions.

slides.title: Probability and Inference
bullets: Introduction to core inferential statistical techniques.
slides.notes: This section introduces the mathematical backbone of statistics, explaining how we quantify uncertainty and calculate risk using probability distributions.

slides.title: Tests and Analysis
slides.notes: Now we move into specific analytical tools, covering everything from categorical variables to basic hypothesis testing and determining statistical power.

slides.title: Advanced ANOVA and Nonparametrics
slides.notes: We explore how to handle complex experimental designs and address scenarios where data may not meet the strict requirements of standard parametric tests.

slides.title: Correlation and Regression
slides.notes: These chapters introduce regression analysis, allowing us to model relationships between continuous variables in clinical and biological research.

slides.title: Complex Modeling
slides.notes: We expand our modeling capabilities to incorporate multiple predictors and explore more flexible, distribution-free analytical techniques.

slides.title: Course Conclusion
bullets: Review of technical appendices and resources.
slides.notes: Finally, we wrap up with supplemental topics and reference materials to support your continued learning in biostatistics.
